---
title: "Results"
---

## Semantic space

All 13,335 artifacts — 8,720 papers and 4,615 iGEM projects — were embedded with
[SPECTER2](https://huggingface.co/allenai/specter2) and projected to two dimensions
using UMAP. The figure below shows the full projection. Blue points are papers;
orange points are iGEM projects; green diamonds are carbon-capture flagged artifacts
from either type.

::: {.callout-note}
Use the legend to toggle artifact types on and off. Hover over any point to see the title, city, and year.
:::

```{=html}
<iframe src="assets/figures/fig1_umap_all.html"
        width="100%" height="620px" frameborder="0"
        style="border-radius:4px; box-shadow: 0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);">
</iframe>
```

**What the projection shows.** Papers and iGEM projects interleave across the semantic
space rather than separating into two distinct blobs. This suggests that the vocabulary
and topics of student projects overlap substantially with those of academic papers —
consistent with the idea that both draw on the same scientific tradition.

---

## Thematic clusters

HDBSCAN identified 347 local clusters in the embedding space. The 25 largest
named clusters (covering about 2,200 artifacts) include:

| Cluster | Size | Example topics |
|---------|-----:|----------------|
| Plastic degradation and biodegradation | 191 | PET, polyhydroxyalkanoates |
| Antimicrobial peptides for crop protection | 189 | bacteriocins, plant immunity |
| Bacteriophage therapy and engineering | 115 | phage display, lytic cycles |
| Microbial chassis and metabolic engineering | 114 | *E. coli*, *B. subtilis* chassis |
| Engineered bacteria for cancer therapy | 110 | tumor-targeting microbes |
| Engineered living materials and assembly | 105 | biofilms, curli fibres |
| CRISPR-Cas genome editing systems | 90 | Cas9, base editing |
| Biofuel and fine chemical production | 79 | ethanol, terpenes, fatty acids |
| Minimal genome design and essentiality | 75 | JCVI-syn3, essential genes |
| DNA assembly automation standards | 73 | Golden Gate, BioBricks, MoClo |

The remaining ~11,000 artifacts fall into smaller thematic clusters (each ≤ 54 artifacts)
collectively labelled "Other".

---

## City-level semantic overlap

For each of the 379 cities with both papers and iGEM projects, we computed the
**cosine similarity** between the average (centroid) SPECTER2 embedding of that city's
papers and the average embedding of its projects. Higher values mean the city's papers
and projects are topically closer.

```{=html}
<iframe src="assets/figures/fig3_city_overlap.html"
        width="100%" height="720px" frameborder="0"
        style="border-radius:4px; box-shadow: 0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);">
</iframe>
```

Overlap scores range from **0.80 to 0.99** (mean 0.94). The range is narrow because
all artifacts are drawn from a single field (synthetic biology), so SPECTER2 places
even diverse documents fairly close together. Variation within this range is nonetheless
real and interpretable: cities at the high end have papers and projects that are
genuinely more co-topical than cities at the low end.

**Top cities** include Cambridge (US), Shanghai, Tianjin, Berkeley, and Boston — all
major synthetic biology research centres with large, focused iGEM programmes.

---

## Regression: what predicts higher overlap?

We regressed city-level semantic overlap on city size (log papers, log projects),
temporal engagement (year span of iGEM activity), and carbon-capture shares.
Standard errors are HC3 heteroskedasticity-robust.

| Variable | (1) Size only | (2) + Temporal | (3) + Carbon |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| log(papers) | 0.0168\*\*\* (0.0015) | 0.0165\*\*\* (0.0015) | 0.0167\*\*\* (0.0015) |
| log(projects) | 0.0187\*\*\* (0.0018) | 0.0102\*\*\* (0.0030) | 0.0102\*\*\* (0.0030) |
| iGEM year span | — | 0.0016\*\*\* (0.0004) | 0.0016\*\*\* (0.0004) |
| CC paper share | — | — | 0.0188 (0.0157) |
| CC project share | — | — | −0.0086 (0.0188) |
| R² | 0.620 | 0.634 | 0.638 |
| N | 379 | 379 | 379 |

*\*\*\* p < 0.01; \*\* p < 0.05; \* p < 0.1. Robust standard errors in parentheses.*

**Key findings:**

1. **More papers and more projects → higher overlap** (Models 1–3). Cities with larger
   synthetic biology footprints show more topical alignment between their papers and projects.
   This likely reflects specialisation: larger research communities tend to focus on a narrower
   set of topics rather than spreading across the whole field.

2. **Longer iGEM engagement → higher overlap** (Models 2–3). Cities where iGEM teams have
   been active over more years show significantly higher overlap, even after controlling for
   project count. This is consistent with the idea that sustained student engagement tracks
   a city's research specialisation rather than being a one-off event.

3. **Carbon-capture shares are not significant** (Model 3). Once size and temporal scope are
   controlled, the share of carbon-capture artifacts does not independently predict higher
   overlap. This may reflect the small size of the carbon-capture subset or the broad semantic
   similarity of all synthetic biology content.

4. **Size explains most variation** (R² ≈ 0.62). The two size variables alone account for
   62% of the variance in city-level overlap, suggesting the primary driver is specialisation
   that comes with scale, not anything specific to iGEM activity patterns.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Causal interpretation
These associations cannot be interpreted causally. Cities with more papers and more projects
are likely the same cities with stronger research infrastructure, more specialised universities,
and larger synthetic biology communities — all factors that independently drive both outcomes.
:::
